text: After exploratory laparoscopic staging, the gastrohepatic ligament is divided and the branches of the celiac trunk are exposed.
A complete lymphadenectomy of the left gastric artery, hepatic artery and celiac trunk is performed.
As you can see, the aforementioned arteries are completely skeletonized.
Each neural station is sent separately to pathology.
The left gastric artery is divided at its origin with a vascular load.
The stomach is completely mobilized by dividing the gastrocolic ligament and the short gastric vessels.
The right gastroepiploic arcade is very carefully preserved.
All the perigastric lymph nodes, including those along the splenic artery, are completely removed.
The distal esophagus is dissected transietally into the lower mediastinum, and a periesophageal Penrose drain is placed within the chest and will be used to facilitate the thoracoscopic dissection.
The stomach is tubularized into a narrow conduit with multiple linear stapling loads.
The most proximal portion of the stomach is not divided to allow conduit retrieval along with the esophagus from the chest.
The suture line is buttressed with non-absorbable interrupted stitches.
Botulinum toxin is injected into the pyloric sphincter to ensure optimal gastric emptying.
The abdominal phase of the procedure is concluded by adding a feeding jejunostomy.
The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided and the mediastinal pleura is open to access the posterior mediastinum.
The azicus vein is encircled and divided to expose the esophagus in its entire length.
The periaesophageal pin rose is retrieved to facilitate periaesophageal dissection.
The esophagus is dissected and blocked with the periaesophageal fat tissue from the airways, the aorta, and the pericardium.
The proximal esophagus is divided and the proximal margin is sent for frozen section.
A complete infracaryonal lymphadenectomy is performed for adenocarcinoma of the distal esophagus.
We add a peritracheal dissection for squamous cell cancers.
An Orville is introduced transorally and placed within the proximal esophageal stump.
The gastric conduit is pulled up into the chest with gentle traction.
The specimen is fully separated from the tubularized stomach and retrieved through a wound protector.
The most proximal portion of the stomach is opened to allow introduction of the circular stapler.
A circular mechanical gastroesophageal anastomosis is performed just proximally to the gastroepiploic arcade.
The tip of the gastric conduit is resected with a linear stapler.
When available, the pleura is used to cover the anastomosis.
